question:
Let $B$ be a set of more than $2^{n+1}/n$ distinct points with
coordinates
of the form $(\pm 1,\pm 1,\ldots,\pm 1)$ in $n$-dimensional space with
$n\geq 3$.
Show that there are three distinct points in $B$ which are the vertices of
an
equilateral triangle.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
solution:
For each point $P$ in $B$, let $S_P$ be the set of points with
all coordinates equal to $\pm 1$ which
differ from $P$ in exactly one coordinate. Since there are more than
$2^{n+1}/n$ points in $B$, and each $S_P$ has $n$ elements, the
cardinalities of the sets $S_P$ add up to more than $2^{n+1}$, which
is to say, more than twice the total number of points. By the
pigeonhole principle, there must be a point in three of the
sets, say $S_P, S_Q, S_R$. But then any two of $P, Q, R$ differ in
exactly two coordinates, so $PQR$ is an equilateral triangle, as
desired.


\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long.question:
Let $pointset$ be a set of more than $2^{dimension+1}/dimension$ distinct points with
coordinates
of the form $(\pm 1,\pm 1,\ldots,\pm 1)$ in $dimension$-dimensional space with
$dimension\geq 3$.
Show that there are three distinct points in $pointset$ which are the vertices of
an
equilateral triangle.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
descriptive_long.solution:
For each point $vertexone$ in $pointset$, let $adjacentp$ be the set of points with
all coordinates equal to $\pm 1$ which
differ from $vertexone$ in exactly one coordinate. Since there are more than
$2^{dimension+1}/dimension$ points in $pointset$, and each $adjacentp$ has $dimension$ elements, the
cardinalities of the sets $adjacentp$ add up to more than $2^{dimension+1}$, which
is to say, more than twice the total number of points. By the
pigeonhole principle, there must be a point in three of the
sets, say $adjacentp, adjacentq, adjacentr$. But then any two of $vertexone, vertextwo, vertexthr$ differ in
exactly two coordinates, so $vertexonevertextwovertexthr$ is an equilateral triangle, as
desired.


\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $blackbird$ be a set of more than $2^{narrowness+1}/narrowness$ distinct points with
coordinates
of the form $(\pm 1,\pm 1,\ldots,\pm 1)$ in $narrowness$-dimensional space with
$narrowness\geq 3$.
Show that there are three distinct points in $blackbird$ which are the vertices of
an
equilateral triangle.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
For each point $pinecone$ in $blackbird$, let $treadmill$ be the set of points with
all coordinates equal to $\pm 1$ which
differ from $pinecone$ in exactly one coordinate. Since there are more than
$2^{narrowness+1}/narrowness$ points in $blackbird$, and each $treadmill$ has $narrowness$ elements, the
cardinalities of the sets $treadmill$ add up to more than $2^{narrowness+1}$, which
is to say, more than twice the total number of points. By the
pigeonhole principle, there must be a point in three of the
sets, say $treadmill, sandstorm, palmolive$. But then any two of $pinecone, quagmire, raincloud$ differ in
exactly two coordinates, so $pinecone quagmire raincloud$ is an equilateral triangle, as
desired.


\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $singleton$ be a set of more than $2^{limitless+1}/limitless$ distinct points with
coordinates
of the form $(\pm 1,\pm 1,\ldots,\pm 1)$ in $limitless$-dimensional space with
$limitless\geq 3$.
Show that there are three distinct points in $singleton$ which are the vertices of
an
equilateral triangle.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
For each point $emptiness$ in $singleton$, let $voidrealm$ be the set of points with
all coordinates equal to $\pm 1$ which
differ from $emptiness$ in exactly one coordinate. Since there are more than
$2^{limitless+1}/limitless$ points in $singleton$, and each $voidrealm$ has $limitless$ elements, the
cardinalities of the sets $voidrealm$ add up to more than $2^{limitless+1}$, which
is to say, more than twice the total number of points. By the
pigeonhole principle, there must be a point in three of the
sets, say $voidrealm, boundless, silentsea$. But then any two of $emptiness, continuum, infinity$ differ in
exactly two coordinates, so $emptinesscontinuuminfinity$ is an equilateral triangle, as
desired.

garbled_string.question:
Let $fjdospwe$ be a set of more than $2^{ghtlbrmq+1}/ghtlbrmq$ distinct points with
coordinates
of the form $(\pm 1,\pm 1,\ldots,\pm 1)$ in $ghtlbrmq$-dimensional space with
$ghtlbrmq\geq 3$.
Show that there are three distinct points in $fjdospwe$ which are the vertices of
an
equilateral triangle.
garbled_string.solution:
For each point $qzxwvtnp$ in $fjdospwe$, let $lwkdmfja$ be the set of points with
all coordinates equal to $\pm 1$ which
differ from $qzxwvtnp$ in exactly one coordinate. Since there are more than
$2^{ghtlbrmq+1}/ghtlbrmq$ points in $fjdospwe$, and each $lwkdmfja$ has $ghtlbrmq$ elements, the
cardinalities of the sets $lwkdmfja$ add up to more than $2^{ghtlbrmq+1}$, which
is to say, more than twice the total number of points. By the
pigeonhole principle, there must be a point in three of the
sets, say $lwkdmfja, zkrptuav, nxyqrsop$. But then any two of $qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mbvcqryu$ differ in
exactly two coordinates, so $qzxwvtnphjgrkslambvcqryu$ is an equilateral triangle, as
desired.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 4$ be an integer.  Consider the $2^{n}$ vertices of the $n$-dimensional unit cube, i.e.
all points of the form \((x_{1},\dots ,x_{n})\) with each $x_{k}\in\{0,1\}$.  
Suppose $B$ is a subset of these vertices with
\[|B|\;>\;\frac{3\,2^{n}}{n}.\]
Prove that $B$ contains three distinct points that are the vertices of an equilateral triangle.
kernel_variant.solution:
For every point P\in B define
S_P = {cube vertices that differ from P in exactly one coordinate}.

1. Each set S_P has exactly n elements, one for each coordinate that can be toggled. Hence
   \sum _{P\in B} |S_P| = n\cdot |B| > n\cdot (3\cdot 2^n / n) = 3\cdot 2^n.

2. The entire cube has only 2^n vertices, so the total just computed exceeds twice that number:
   3\cdot 2^n > 2\cdot 2^n = 2^{n+1}.
   By the pigeonhole principle, some vertex V must belong to at least three distinct sets, say
   V \in  S_P \cap  S_Q \cap  S_R with P, Q, R \in  B all distinct.

3. The vertex V differs from each of P, Q, R in exactly one coordinate.  Because P\neq Q, the coordinates in which P and Q differ from V must be different; consequently P and Q differ in exactly two coordinates.  The same reasoning applies to the pairs (Q,R) and (R,P).

4. In the unit cube, two vertices that differ in exactly two coordinates are at distance \sqrt{2} apart.  Therefore
   |PQ| = |QR| = |RP| = \sqrt{2},
   so the triangle PQR is equilateral.

Thus B indeed contains three vertices that form an equilateral triangle.

core_steps: For every P in B, form the set S_P of the n cube-vertices that differ from P in exactly one coordinate.
Since the whole cube has only 2^n vertices, the pigeonhole principle forces some vertex to lie in at least three different S_P sets.
Those three P’s all differ pairwise in exactly two coordinates, giving equal Euclidean distances—hence an equilateral triangle.

slot1.description: Lower bound on |B| chosen so that n·|B| exceeds twice the number of cube vertices; any constant c>2 would work.

slot2.description: Actual numerical values assigned to cube coordinates; any two opposite numbers (e.g. 0 and 1, or ±a) preserve the argument.

slot3.description: Minimal dimension explicitly stated in the problem; any requirement n ≥ 3 (or any stricter n ≥ k with k ≥ 3) leaves the proof unchanged.
